translation.content: Minas Gerais isn't the typical Brazil of postcards. The southeastern state knows no endless beaches or swaying coconut palms. Yet from this landlocked terrain emerged one of its most accomplished sons. As OMOLOKO, João Vitor has mastered the art of summoning summer on the dance floor. Armed with a pair of CDJs and a USB, he carries sun-kissed house dreams shaped by countless hours lost in Discogs rabbit holes, forgotten corners of YouTube and the dust of hidden record shops. Vitor was born in Rio Grande do Norte, in Brazil's northeast, before moving south as a child when his family set out in search of new opportunities—a well-worn path in the world's fifth-largest country. Adopting the alias OMOLOKO in the late 2010s, he quickly became a beacon in Belo Horizonte's bubbling electronic scene. Carrying sounds from home deep in his memory alongside a restless desire to make the world dance to his own findings, he carved out a singular voice with genre-hopping sets, grounded in an affection for infectious grooves and warm, rolling kicks. In recent years, Vitor's fine-tuning of his craft behind the decks have made him more than a familiar face at countless essential nightlife hubs around the world, from Panorama Bar to Dekmantel, São Paulo's Gop Tun to Ibiza's DC-10. His résumé, already impressive, is expanding nicely. So to mark the beginning of carnaval in Brazil, who better for RA.1025. Vitor's RA Mix draws deeply from the lineage of house's most celebrated names, alongside obscure gems your Shazam wouldn't dare recognise (believe me, I've tried). With slow-cooking patience, the session follows wherever the language of dance leads: South African kwaito, diva vocal flashes, funk-laced deep house, vibraphone-led strides and salsa-laced drumwork. It’s like a dream team of house offshoots, all meeting for the very first time at the beach. -Felipe Maia How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded on VirtualDJ, honey! Best software on the market. Well, I'm a Latin American DJ, so I used to record with what I have in my hands. I don't have CDJs at home, but I have creativity, and VirtualDJ software is where I learned how to play. It's where I learned how to mix. It's a software that has always helped me since I started DJing, and that's it—I recorded the set using my fingers, you know, mixing it on my laptop. So that was the main equipment that I used. And I think that's fun, because it's a software that really helps people who don't have access to CDJs. The idea behind it? That's a hard question, because I'm always up for everything. I'm a music nerd, and I usually follow what moves me emotionally. But I'm a house girl—I can't deny I love to listen to house from all over the world. Lately, I'm diving really deep into deep house, some sexy, groovy deep house. This mix is also about trying to create a story that is very atmospheric in the beginning and then gradually getting more groovy and a little bit sassy. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm not a person who likes to think about the future that much—if I think too much about the future, I'm gonna get crazy. I'm just really glad that I'm doing what I'm doing, that I'm able to travel the world, meet people, and take my work and music all over. This gives me inspiration and happiness, and that gives me validation to keep doing what I'm doing, and to be able to be a Latin American DJ, doing art in Brazil and still being able to travel and have the opportunity to live this life. It's something that really brings me joy. Also, I love to keep doing what I'm doing and hopefully buy my mother's house and my house—that would be amazing! What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I mean, we live in a crazy world, but I just wish we paid more attention to class divisions in the quiet afterlife of colonialism. So many injustices feel invisible because they have been normalised for generations. And when we start noticing the circles of who benefits and who's left out, and where stories came from, we open space for more care, kindness and shared humanity. When we start to think from this point, I think then we're going to be able to change things. That's it. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? One club that shaped my career was Deputamadre Club in Belo Horizonte. I lived there for ten years. It was where I created my community and my parties. And right now, a club that really warms my heart is in the Northeast of Brazil, in the state where I was born. I'm from the Northeast. It's called Clube Frisson. Every time that I go there and play there, it's so special. That's it.
translation.blurb: The Brazilian party starter unveils 60 minutes of sun-drenched house.
